title: Structural Colours with Transition Metal Dichalcogenide Nanostructures
authors: Ida Juliane Bundgaard, Catarina G. Ferreira, Yonas Lebsir, Christos Tserkezis
abstract: We introduce transition metal-dichalcogenide (TMD) nanostructures as a promising platform for the realisation of structural colours. Processing of semianalytically calculated reflectance spectra of TMD nanosphere arrays shows a wide range of colours, which are obtained simply through tailoring the radius and separation of spheres in the array, with the size-dependent Mie modes of the nanoparticles being the primary contributor to the spectra. Additionally, it is demonstrated that further coverage of the colour space can be obtained by employing different materials or different lattice unit cells. Theoretical examination of the impact of the excitonic attributes of TMDs on the resulting structural colours indicates that self-hybridisation between nanoparticle modes and excitonic transitions may be employed for further tuneability. Moreover, the impact of TMD anisotropy on the structural colours is shown to be negligible for small structures at typical viewing angles, while the viewing angle itself may impact the colour. This work sets out to be a general investigation of TMD nanoarchitectures, with a focus on nanosphere arrays, for structural colours, by examining both inherent material features through the lens of colourimetry, and the ability of such structures to sustain a broad range of hues.
summary: This paper explores how TMD nanostructures can be engineered to produce a wide variety of structural colours. By adjusting the size and arrangement of TMD nanoparticles, researchers can manipulate Mie resonances and lattice modes to cover a large portion of the visible RGB spectrum. The study also highlights how material-specific features, like excitonic transitions, can be used to further tune the optical response. This work provides a roadmap for designing highly tunable, nanostructured optical surfaces.
main_problem: Investigating the potential of Transition Metal Dichalcogenide (TMD) nanostructures, specifically nanoparticle arrays, as a platform for generating tunable structural colours.
main_result: TMD nanosphere arrays can achieve a wide range of colours by tailoring particle radius and lattice separation, with expanded colour space coverage possible through different TMD materials and bipartite array configurations.
method: Semi-analytical calculations using the T-matrix approach, Mie theory, and lattice sums, combined with colorimetric analysis (CIE XYZ/Lab) to evaluate perceived colour.
model_or_system: Arrays of spherical TMD nanoparticles (such as WS2, MoS2, and HfSe2) in square and bipartite lattices, as well as thin anisotropic TMD slabs.
key_observables: Reflectance spectra, scattering and absorption cross-sections, CIE XYZ tristimulus values, and color difference (Delta E_Lab).
important_parameters: Particle radius (30-150 nm), lattice constant (up to 800 nm), incidence angle, material type, and slab thickness.
main_assumptions: Nanoparticles are treated as isotropic for small structures; relative permeability is assumed to be unity; spherical geometries are used as a first approximation for more complex morphologies.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows permittivity and cross-sections for different models; Figure 3 displays reflectance spectra and colours for various radii and lattice constants; Figure 4 shows angular dependence; Figure 5 compares different array configurations; Figure 6 presents CIE colour space plots showing coverage expansion.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of TMD-based structural colours, details the electromagnetic modeling and colorimetric methods, analyzes the impact of particle geometry and material properties (including excitons and anisotropy), explores array configurations for maximizing colour gamut, and concludes with potential extensions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Illustration of the differences between a model of a lossless HID compared to a toy model, with the same base permittivity with one Lorentzian added to represent an exciton at 600 nm. Panel (a) shows the real (full lines) and imaginary part (dashed lines) of the permittivities of the plain model (M1, dark blue line) and the toy model (M2, light blue line). Panel (b) shows the normalised scattering cross sections, σsc, of a single NP of the array for both the plain model (M1, dark blue line) and the toy model (M2, light blue line) as well as the normalised absorption cross section, σabs, of the toy model (M2, red line). The plain model exhibits no absorption and its σabs is thus...

Figure 2. Visualisations of the investigation of a WS2 slab. (a) Reflectance of TE and TM light impringing on a T = 100 nm slab, illustrated by the sketch in (b), when assumed isotropic or anisotropic for three incidence angles, θ. (c) Reflectance of TM light at θ = 45◦for varying thicknesses when the slab is assumed isotropic or anisotropic. (d) Colours stemming from the reflectance spectra for isotropic WS2 slabs, with thicknesses varying from 52 nm to 150 nm in steps of 2 nm, as indicated by the numbers below each square.

Figure 3. Visualisations of data obtained from arrays of spherical NPs. Panels (a), (b), and (c) show reflectance spectra and corresponding colours for infinite arrays of spherical NPs, with variable lattice constants and radii of (a) 90 nm, (b) 110 nm, and (c) 150 nm. Panel (d) shows the normalised scattering cross section of finite arrays of varying sizes and the reflectance spectra of an infinite array of spherical WS2 NPs, with radius r = 100 nm and lattice constant a = 700 nm. The inset in each subpanel shows the colours of a single NP and the colour of the infinite array, respectively.

Figure 4. Colours obtained from selected arrays with NPs of radius (a) 90 nm (b) 110 nm and (c) 150 nm with a variation of incidence angle. Each panel contains the colours from the TE, TM and total (average of TM and TE) reflectances as the angle of incidence is varied from 0◦to 45◦. (d) The colour difference, ∆ELab, between the average colour at each incidence angle with respect to the colour at normal incidence.

Figure 5. Colours obtained from arrays with NPs of radius (a) 70 nm, (b) 100 nm and (c) the combined bipartite array at the same lattice constants varying from a1 = 260 nm to a12 = 480 nm in steps of 20 nm. An illustration of the configuration of particles in the bipartite array can be seen in (d).

Figure 6. Plots of the CIE colour space, where each white dot represents a colour obtained by one of the arrays investigated. Subfigure (a) show arrays consisting of spherical WS2 NPs, where the radius of the particles vary from 30 nm to 150 nm in steps of 10 nm, while the lattice constant varies from the diameter of the particles to 800 nm in steps of 20 nm. In (b) arrays with intermediate radii in steps of two have been added as well as selected bipartite arrays. In subfigure (c) arrays of the same structure as the arrays in (a) but utilising several different TMDs, here MoS2[57], MoSe2[58], HfS2[59] and HfSe2[58].
